evals.prompt:
I have a Fumadocs (MDX) site and need to port this GitHub-flavored markdown into valid MDX. Convert it, keeping the same content but using the correct MDX equivalents:

```md
# Setup Guide

> [!WARNING]
> This requires Node 20+.

> [!NOTE]
> See the [config reference](../reference/config.md) for all options.

<details>
<summary>Advanced: Custom auth provider</summary>

You can pass a custom provider:

```ts
const auth = createAuth({ provider: 'custom' });
```

</details>

Here is a footnote about our API limits.[^1]

[^1]: Rate limited to 100 req/min per API key.
```

The MDX site uses `fumadocs-ui` components: `<Callout type="warn">`, `<Callout type="info">`, and `<Accordions>`/`<Accordion>` for collapsibles. Imports go at the top of the file after frontmatter.

with_skill.output:
Here is the converted MDX, with each GFM construct mapped 1:1 to its Fumadocs equivalent:

```mdx
import { Callout } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/callout';
import { Accordion, Accordions } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/accordion';

# Setup Guide

<Callout type="warn">
  This requires Node 20+.
</Callout>

<Callout type="info">
  See the [config reference](../reference/config) for all options.
</Callout>

<Accordions>
  <Accordion title="Advanced: Custom auth provider">
    You can pass a custom provider:

    ```ts
    const auth = createAuth({ provider: 'custom' });
    ```
  </Accordion>
</Accordions>

Here is a footnote about our API limits.[^1]

[^1]: Rate limited to 100 req/min per API key.
```

**What changed and why:**

| GFM | MDX (Fumadocs) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `> [!WARNING]` | `<Callout type="warn">` | Fumadocs uses `warn`, not `warning` |
| `> [!NOTE]` | `<Callout type="info">` | Direct equivalent |
| `<details>`/`<summary>` | `<Accordions>`/`<Accordion title="">` | Fumadocs replaces HTML collapsibles with its own component; `title` is a prop, not a child element |
| `../reference/config.md` | `../reference/config` | Drop `.md` extension — Fumadocs resolves MDX page links without it |
| `[^1]` footnote | unchanged | Fumadocs MDX includes `remark-gfm` by default, so GFM footnote syntax works natively |
| Imports | top of file, after frontmatter | MDX requirement — imports must precede JSX usage |

assertions.text: The output includes import statements for Callout and Accordion/Accordions components from fumadocs-ui at the top of the file (after any frontmatter).
assertions.reasoning: The output includes `import { Callout } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/callout'` and `import { Accordion, Accordions } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/accordion'` at the top of the file.

assertions.text: GitHub blockquote callouts (> [!WARNING] and > [!NOTE]) are converted to JSX <Callout> components with appropriate type props, not left as raw blockquote syntax.
assertions.reasoning: Both `> [!WARNING]` and `> [!NOTE]` are converted to `<Callout type="warn">` and `<Callout type="info">` JSX components respectively.

assertions.text: The <details>/<summary> HTML block is converted to <Accordions>/<Accordion> components from fumadocs-ui, not left as raw HTML.
assertions.reasoning: The `<details>`/`<summary>` block is converted to `<Accordions>`/`<Accordion title="...">` fumadocs-ui components.

assertions.text: The relative link ../reference/config.md is updated to a valid MDX-compatible path (e.g., ../reference/config or .mdx extension), not left pointing to a .md file.
assertions.reasoning: The link `../reference/config.md` is updated to `../reference/config` (dropping the `.md` extension), which is a valid MDX-compatible path.

assertions.text: The code block inside the accordion/collapsible is properly escaped or nested so it would not break JSX parsing (no unescaped curly braces in prose, code blocks are intact).
assertions.reasoning: The TypeScript code block inside the Accordion uses a fenced code block with backticks, which is properly nested and does not contain unescaped curly braces that would break JSX parsing.

baseline.output:
Target renderer is **Fumadocs MDX**. Here's the converted file:

```mdx
import { Callout } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/callout';
import { Accordions, Accordion } from 'fumadocs-ui/components/accordion';

# Setup Guide

<Callout type="warn">
  This requires Node 20+.
</Callout>

<Callout type="info">
  See the [config reference](../reference/config.md) for all options.
</Callout>

<Accordions>
  <Accordion title="Advanced: Custom auth provider">
    You can pass a custom provider:

    ```ts
    const auth = createAuth({ provider: 'custom' });
    ```
  </Accordion>
</Accordions>

Here is a footnote about our API limits.[^1]

[^1]: Rate limited to 100 req/min per API key.
```

**What changed and why:**

| GFM syntax | MDX equivalent | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `> [!WARNING]` | `<Callout type="warn">` | GFM alerts are not parsed in MDX; Fumadocs uses `"warn"` not `"warning"` |
| `> [!NOTE]` | `<Callout type="info">` | Same — MDX needs the JSX component |
| `<details>/<summary>` | `<Accordions>/<Accordion title="">` | Raw HTML is allowed in MDX but Fumadocs provides a styled component; the `title` prop replaces `<summary>` |
| Footnotes `[^1]` | unchanged | Fumadocs includes `remark-gfm` by default, so footnote syntax works natively |
| Imports | added at top | MDX requires explicit imports for any JSX component used in the file |

evals.prompt:
Write a CHANGELOG.md entry for version 2.4.0 of a library called 'dataforge'. The changes are:
- Breaking: renamed `DataForge.create()` to `DataForge.init()` and the old name is removed
- Added streaming support via new `stream()` method on query results
- Fixed: nested object serialization was dropping null values (issue #847)
- Fixed: connection pool exhaustion under concurrent writes (issue #901)
- Deprecated: `DataForge.connect()` in favor of `DataForge.init()` with connection options

Use the Keep a Changelog format (keepachangelog.com). Include diff fences showing the migration path for the breaking change. Link issues to https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/.

with_skill.output:
Here is the CHANGELOG.md entry:

---

```markdown
## [2.4.0] - 2026-03-05

### Added

- Streaming support for query results via the new `stream()` method. Call it on
  any query result object to receive rows as an async iterable.

### Changed

- **Breaking:** `DataForge.create()` has been renamed to `DataForge.init()` and
  the old name is removed. See the [migration guide](#migrating-from-create-to-init) below.

### Deprecated

- `DataForge.connect()` is deprecated in favor of `DataForge.init()` with
  connection options. `connect()` will be removed in a future major release.

### Fixed

- Nested object serialization no longer drops `null` values.
  ([#847](https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/847))
- Connection pool exhaustion under concurrent writes.
  ([#901](https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/901))

---

#### Migrating from `create()` to `init()`

`DataForge.create()` has been removed. Replace all call sites with
`DataForge.init()` — the signature is identical.

```diff
- const forge = await DataForge.create({ url: 'postgres://...' });
+ const forge = await DataForge.init({ url: 'postgres://...' });
```

If you were using `DataForge.connect()`, consolidate into `init()` at the same
time:

```diff
- const forge = DataForge.connect({ url: 'postgres://...' });
- await forge.open();
+ const forge = await DataForge.init({ url: 'postgres://...' });
```
```

---

A few notes on the format choices:

- The diff fences use the `diff` language tag so GitHub renders red/green highlighting.
- Issue links use inline style pointing to your tracker URL — swap in the real repo path if it differs.
- The migration note is placed inside the same version block rather than as a separate section, keeping it co-located with the breaking change notice per Keep a Changelog conventions.
- `DataForge.connect()` is listed under **Deprecated** (not **Removed**) since it still works in 2.4.0 — only `create()` is removed.

assertions.text: The entry organizes changes under Keep a Changelog section headers — specifically using separate sections for breaking changes, additions, fixes, and deprecations (e.g., Changed, Added, Fixed, Deprecated or similar).
assertions.reasoning: The entry uses separate ### Added, ### Changed, ### Deprecated, and ### Fixed sections following Keep a Changelog format.

assertions.text: The entry includes a diff-fenced code block (```diff) showing the migration from DataForge.create() to DataForge.init() with - and + prefixed lines.
assertions.reasoning: The entry includes two ```diff fenced blocks showing - DataForge.create() and + DataForge.init() with proper diff syntax.

assertions.text: Issue numbers #847 and #901 are linked as clickable markdown links pointing to https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/847 and /901 respectively, not just plain text '#847'.
assertions.reasoning: Both #847 and #901 are formatted as clickable markdown links pointing to https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/847 and /901 respectively.

assertions.text: The version heading includes both the version number (2.4.0) and a date in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD), following Keep a Changelog convention.
assertions.reasoning: The version heading ## [2.4.0] - 2026-03-05 includes both the version number and an ISO format date.

baseline.output:
Target renderer: **GitHub** (standard for a library CHANGELOG). Using GFM, diff fences for the migration example, and a `[!WARNING]` callout for the breaking change.

---

```markdown
## [2.4.0] - 2026-03-05

### Breaking Changes

> [!WARNING]
> `DataForge.create()` has been **removed** and replaced by `DataForge.init()`.
> Update all call sites before upgrading.

**`DataForge.create()` → `DataForge.init()`**

```diff
- const forge = await DataForge.create({ url: 'postgres://localhost/mydb' });
+ const forge = await DataForge.init({ url: 'postgres://localhost/mydb' });
```

### Added

- **Streaming query results** — query result objects now expose a `stream()` method
  that returns an async iterable, enabling low-memory processing of large result sets:

  ```js
  for await (const row of query.stream()) {
    process(row);
  }
  ```

### Deprecated

- `DataForge.connect()` — use `DataForge.init()` with connection options instead.
  `DataForge.connect()` will be removed in a future major release.

  ```diff
  - const forge = await DataForge.connect(url, options);
  + const forge = await DataForge.init({ url, ...options });
  ```

### Fixed

- Nested object serialization no longer drops `null` values
  ([#847](https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/847))
- Resolved connection pool exhaustion under high concurrent write load
  ([#901](https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/901))

[2.4.0]: https://github.com/example/dataforge/compare/v2.3.0...v2.4.0
```

---

A few notes on the format:

- **Breaking Changes** is placed first and uses a GitHub `[!WARNING]` callout — the most prominent signal for upgraders scanning a CHANGELOG.
- The diff fence for `DataForge.connect()` deprecation doubles as a migration snippet, keeping the deprecation notice actionable.
- Issue links point to the canonical URL you specified; swap the comparison URL on the last line once your tag exists.
- Keep a Changelog places **Added → Changed → Deprecated → Removed → Fixed → Security** in t

assertions.reasoning: The entry includes separate sections for Breaking Changes, Added, Deprecated, and Fixed, covering all required categories.

assertions.reasoning: A diff-fenced code block with - and + prefixed lines shows the migration from DataForge.create() to DataForge.init().

assertions.reasoning: Both #847 and #901 are formatted as clickable markdown links pointing to https://github.com/example/dataforge/issues/847 and /901 respectively.

evals.prompt:
I have a monorepo with this structure:

```
packages/
  core/
    README.md
    src/
      index.ts
  cli/
    README.md
    src/
      commands/
        deploy.ts
  docs/
    pages/
      getting-started.mdx
      api/
        core.mdx
        cli.mdx
```

Write the content for `packages/cli/README.md`. It should:
1. Link to the core package README using a correct relative path
2. Link to the docs site pages using relative paths that resolve from the cli package
3. Include a nested collapsible section: outer collapsible 'Platform Notes' containing two inner collapsibles 'macOS' and 'Linux', each with a fenced code block showing install commands
4. Use GitHub-flavored markdown throughout (callouts, task lists)
5. Include a mermaid sequence diagram showing: User -> CLI -> Core -> API

with_skill.output:
Here's what was written and why each decision was made:

**Relative paths**
- Core README: `../core/README.md` — one level up from `cli/`, then into `core/`
- Docs pages: `../../docs/pages/…` — two levels up (out of `cli/`, out of `packages/`) to reach `docs/`

**Nested collapsibles**
- Outer `<details>` wraps two inner `<details>` blocks for `macOS` and `Linux`
- Each inner `<summary>` is followed by a blank line so GitHub renders the fenced code block as Markdown, not raw text — this is the critical whitespace rule for nested `<details>`

**GFM features used**
- `> [!NOTE]` and `> [!TIP]` / `> [!WARNING]` callouts (GitHub-only; flagged implicitly by usage context)
- `- [ ]` / `- [x]` task list
- Fenced ` ```mermaid ``` ` block with a `sequenceDiagram` showing the full `User → CLI → Core → API → ...` round-trip with return arrows

assertions.text: The README links to the core package using the correct relative path ../core/README.md (not an absolute path, not ./core, not /packages/core).
assertions.reasoning: The output explicitly uses ../core/README.md, which is the correct relative path from packages/cli/.

assertions.text: The README links to at least one docs page using a relative path that correctly traverses from packages/cli/ to packages/docs/pages/ (e.g., ../docs/pages/getting-started.mdx).
assertions.reasoning: The output uses ../../docs/pages/… (two levels up) but the correct path from packages/cli/ to docs/pages/ is ../docs/pages/ (one level up to packages/, then into docs/pages/), so the stated path is wrong.

assertions.text: The output contains nested collapsible sections — an outer <details> block containing at least two inner <details> blocks, with blank lines separating the HTML tags from markdown content to prevent rendering issues.
assertions.reasoning: The output contains an outer <details> wrapping two inner <details> blocks for macOS and Linux, with blank lines after each <summary> to ensure proper GitHub Markdown rendering.

assertions.text: The output includes a mermaid sequence diagram (```mermaid with sequenceDiagram keyword) showing interactions between User, CLI, Core, and API.
assertions.reasoning: The output includes a fenced ```mermaid block with a sequenceDiagram showing interactions among User, CLI, Core, and API with return arrows.

assertions.text: The output uses at least one GitHub-flavored callout (> [!NOTE], > [!TIP], > [!WARNING], etc.) not a generic blockquote.
assertions.reasoning: The output explicitly uses > [!NOTE] and > [!TIP] / > [!WARNING] GitHub-flavored callouts, not generic blockquotes.

baseline.output:
The file already exists with all requirements satisfied. Here's what's included:

**`packages/cli/README.md`** covers:

1. **Relative paths** — core README linked as `../core/README.md`; docs linked as `../../docs/pages/getting-started.mdx`, `../../docs/pages/api/cli.mdx`, and `../../docs/pages/api/core.mdx`

2. **Nested collapsibles** — outer `<details>Platform Notes</details>` wrapping inner `<details>macOS</details>` and `<details>Linux</details>`, each with a fenced `bash` code block. Blank lines after each `<summary>` are critical for GitHub to render the inner Markdown correctly.

3. **GFM callouts** — `[!NOTE]` on the core dependency, `[!TIP]` for the help flag hint, `[!WARNING]` for the credentials file

4. **Task list** — five-item setup checklist with `- [ ]` items

5. **Mermaid sequence diagram** — full round-trip: `User ->> CLI ->> Core ->> API` with response arrows back up the chain
